metadata.summary: Production Rails patterns: service objects, query objects, and Sidekiq jobs for scalability and maintainability.

content.title: Ruby on Rails Backend Development
content.summary: Build production-grade Rails applications by extracting multi-step business logic into Service Objects, using Query Objects for efficient database access, and leveraging Sidekiq for asynchronous processing. Wrap multi-step writes in transactions, move side effects outside transactions, and use RSpec with FactoryBot for comprehensive testing.

why.p: Fat controllers and fat models with callbacks lead to unpredictable execution order and test coupling. Service Objects provide a uniform success/failure contract without exception sniffing. Query Objects decouple database access from business logic and enable chainable, reusable queries. Sidekiq with proper retry policies and dead-letter handling prevents message loss and ensures idempotency.

The most dangerous anti-pattern is calling HTTP or sending emails mid-transaction: a rolled-back insert leaves a sent email or a charged card. Proper separation of transaction boundaries from side effects prevents race conditions and data inconsistencies.

list.item: Rails 7+ applications where controllers or ActiveRecord callbacks have grown unwieldy.
Multi-step writes that must be transactional plus side effects (mailers, audit logs, webhooks).
Applications needing a uniform success/failure return shape so controllers do not sniff exceptions.
Refactoring fat controllers and fat models toward service-centric architecture.
Asynchronous processing of slow workflows with Sidekiq jobs and retry policies.
Tiny single-step writes where wrapping in a service is excessive ceremony.
Read-only endpoints where services add no value over a query object or scope.
Apps using Hanami, Sinatra, or pure Rack where Rails conventions do not transfer.
High-throughput message processing (>10k msg/s) where Sidekiq hits ceiling; use Karafka or dedicated Go/Rust worker.
Rails 5/6 LTS where some patterns (after_create_commit, encrypted attributes) require Rails 7+.

section.name: service-objects
section.p: Extract multi-step business logic into Service Objects that accept params and current_user in initialize and return a single canonical ServiceResult from #call. Wrap multi-step writes in ActiveRecord::Base.transaction. Move mailers, webhooks, and HTTP calls to after_commit callbacks or a transactional outbox.

rule.statement: Define exactly one ServiceResult at app/services/service_result.rb. All service objects return it from #call. CI must fail if a second definition is found. Use success?/failure? as the only API.
rule.rationale: LLMs invent slightly different ServiceResult shapes per service file. A divergent API means every controller must handle multiple shapes. Lock the API in CI with a simple grep for class ServiceResult.

rule.statement: Service objects accept params: and current_user: in initialize. The public API is #call returning ServiceResult. All private helpers are private. Namespace by domain: Users::CreateService.
rule.rationale: A consistent interface means every service is discoverable and can be tested the same way. Namespacing by domain avoids collisions and makes the service directory navigable.

rule.statement: Move mailers, webhook HTTP calls, and search index updates to after_commit callbacks, an outbox, or chained Sidekiq jobs. Never call deliver_later or external HTTP inside ActiveRecord::Base.transaction.
rule.rationale: deliver_later inside a transaction enqueues the job before the transaction commits. If the transaction rolls back, the worker runs against a non-existent record. The race window is small but real under load.

rule.statement: Controllers call params.require(...).permit(...) before passing to the service. Services receive an already-permitted hash. Services must not call params directly.
rule.rationale: Strong Parameters enforce an allowlist at the HTTP boundary. Services receiving a raw params object bypass the allowlist, enabling mass assignment attacks on any field the service writes.

example.title: ServiceResult Class
Service Object with Transaction
example.code:
# app/services/users/create_service.rb module Users class CreateService def initialize(params:, current_user: nil) @params = params @current_user = current_user end
def call user = User.new(user_params)
ActiveRecord::Base.transaction do user.save! create_audit_log(user) end
# Side effects AFTER commit UserMailer.welcome(user).deliver_later ServiceResult.success(user) rescue ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid => e ServiceResult.failure(e.record.errors.full_messages) end
private
def user_params @params.slice(:name, :email, :password) end
def create_audit_log(user) AuditLog.create!( action: 'user.created', resource: user, actor: @current_user ) end end end

example.title: Controller Using Service
example.code:
# app/controllers/api/v1/users_controller.rb module Api module V1 class UsersController < ApplicationController def create result = Users::CreateService.new( params: user_params, current_user: current_user ).call
if result.success? render json: UserSerializer.new(result.data), status: :created else render json: { errors: result.errors }, status: :unprocessable_entity end end
private
def user_params params.require(:user).permit(:name, :email, :password) end end end end

section.name: query-objects
section.p: Use Query Objects to decouple database access from business logic. Query Objects accept a relation and return chainable builders that apply filters, sorts, and eager-loads incrementally. They make N+1 detection easier and enable reusable, testable query logic.

rule.statement: Query objects take an optional relation and return self from filter methods to enable chaining. Provide a #results method that returns the final ActiveRecord::Relation.
rule.rationale: Chainable builder pattern matches the fluent style of ActiveRecord scopes and keeps query assembly readable. Returning self enables functional composition without intermediate variables.

example.title: Query Object

section.name: activerecord-patterns
section.p: Use scopes for reusable filtering logic, validations for data integrity, and callbacks only for invariants. Avoid callbacks for business logic; push that into services.

rule.statement: Use before_validation for invariants only: normalizing email, formatting phone numbers. Never use callbacks for business logic like mailers or webhooks.
rule.rationale: Callbacks are invisible and run in unpredictable order; business logic in callbacks is hard to test and reason about in services. Callbacks for invariants are acceptable because they preserve data constraints.

rule.statement: Use after_create_commit for side effects that need the row persisted (mailers, jobs). Never use after_save for these.
rule.rationale: after_create_commit runs only after the transaction commits, guaranteeing the row exists. after_save runs inside the transaction and can roll back, leaving side effects dangling.

example.title: Model with Scopes, Validations, and Callbacks
example.code:
# app/models/user.rb class User < ApplicationRecord # Associations has_one :profile, dependent: :destroy has_many :orders, dependent: :nullify has_many :roles, through: :user_roles
# Validations validates :email, presence: true, uniqueness: { case_sensitive: false } validates :name, presence: true, length: { minimum: 2, maximum: 100 }
# Scopes scope :active, -> { where(active: true) } scope :admins, -> { joins(:roles).where(roles: { name: 'admin' }) } scope :recent, -> { where('created_at > ?', 30.days.ago) }
# Callbacks before_validation :normalize_email after_create_commit :send_welcome_email
private
def normalize_email self.email = email&.downcase&.strip end
def send_welcome_email UserMailer.welcome(self).deliver_later end end

section.name: sidekiq-background-jobs
section.p: Use Sidekiq for asynchronous processing of slow workflows. Configure retry policies, set up dead-letter queues, and batch-process large datasets efficiently. Always pass IDs to jobs, not objects.

rule.statement: Sidekiq jobs accept primitives (IDs, strings) — never AR objects. Load fresh records inside perform to avoid stale state serialization.
rule.rationale: AR objects serialize as YAML and load a potentially stale record when the worker runs. Passing IDs ensures the worker always fetches the latest state.

rule.statement: Configure retry: <N> and dead: true on every job. Use sidekiq_retry_in to define backoff strategy per exception type.
rule.rationale: Missing retry counts cause message loss on transient errors. Dead-letter handling surfaces permanent failures for investigation.

example.title: Sidekiq Job with Retry Policy
example.code:
# app/jobs/process_order_job.rb class ProcessOrderJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :default, retry: 3, dead: true
sidekiq_retry_in do |count, exception| case exception when PaymentGatewayError (count + 1) * 60 # Linear backoff for payment issues else (count ** 4) + 15 # Exponential backoff end end
def perform(order_id) order = Order.find(order_id)
return if order.processed?
OrderProcessor.new(order).process!
NotifyCustomerJob.perform_async(order_id) rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound # Order deleted, nothing to do Sidekiq.logger.warn "Order #{order_id} not found" rescue PaymentGatewayError => e # Will retry with custom backoff raise rescue StandardError => e ErrorTracker.capture(e, order_id: order_id) raise end end

example.title: Batch Processing with find_each
example.code:
# app/jobs/batch_export_job.rb class BatchExportJob include Sidekiq::Job
sidekiq_options queue: :exports, retry: 1
def perform(export_id) export = Export.find(export_id) export.update!(status: :processing)
records = export.query_records
CSV.open(export.file_path, 'wb') do |csv| csv << export.headers records.find_each(batch_size: 1000) do |record| csv << export.row_for(record) end end
export.update!(status: :completed, completed_at: Time.current) ExportMailer.ready(export).deliver_later rescue StandardError => e export.update!(status: :failed, error_message: e.message) raise end end

section.name: rspec-testing
section.p: Write comprehensive tests covering models, services, and request specs. Use factory_bot for test data, shoulda-matchers for model validations, and explicit setup blocks for clarity.

rule.statement: One describe per public method, one context per branch, one it per assertion.
rule.rationale: Diagnostic failure messages: "Users::CreateService#call with valid params creates a user" pinpoints behavior without reading test body.

rule.statement: Prefer build_stubbed over create for model tests. Use create only when persistence is required.
rule.rationale: build_stubbed skips the database; model unit tests are 10-100x faster with no schema dependency.

example.title: Model Spec with Shoulda-Matchers
Service Spec

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when LLM agents generate Rails code.

antipattern.description: Controllers call User.create(params) directly instead of using a service object.
antipattern.reason: The methodology mandates service objects. Reject this in review. Controllers are HTTP handlers, not business logic.

antipattern.description: Agents emit before_save callbacks that mutate state in surprising ways and break tests.
antipattern.reason: Callbacks run in unpredictable order. Prefer explicit service-layer logic with one public #call method. Use callbacks only for invariants.

antipattern.description: Sidekiq retry counts and dead: true flags are mistyped. The agent must read sidekiq.yml and existing job classes to align with team conventions.
antipattern.reason: Missing retry configuration causes message loss. Inconsistent retry policies across jobs are hard to debug.

antipattern.description: params.permit(...) is often forgotten or too permissive. Controllers pass raw params; services do not enforce allowlist.
antipattern.reason: CI must enforce strong parameters. Mass assignment attacks otherwise possible on any field the service writes.

antipattern.description: N+1 queries proliferate in serializers and views. Bullet catches them in dev/test only if enabled.
antipattern.reason: Agent must enable Bullet before generating views/serializers. N+1 is invisible until load testing.

antipattern.description: Rails 7's encrypted attributes need bin/rails db:encryption:init first. Agents skip this and produce migrations that crash in CI.
antipattern.reason: Encrypted attributes require setup. Review migrations before applying to non-dev environments.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop checkpoint: any migration with remove_column, rename_column, or down block must be reviewed before db:migrate in non-dev envs.
rule.rationale: Irreversible migrations cannot be rolled back. Agents generate destructive migrations without checking if the column has data.
